does: This paper empirically investigates linguistic discrimination in large language models by measuring inconsistency in model outputs across different languages. The authors evaluate safety and response quality using two datasets (AdvBench and NQ) on four LLMs (Llama2-13b, Gemma-7b, GPT-3.5-turbo, Gemini-pro). Their core finding demonstrates stark disparities in safety alignment across languages: high-resource languages like English, French, Russian, and Spanish show only 1.04% jailbreak success rates on average, while low-resource languages including Bengali, Georgian, Nepali, and Maithili exhibit 27.7% jailbreak success rates. This asymmetry reveals that LLMs maintain consistency in refusing harmful queries primarily for well-represented languages while exhibiting degraded safety guardrails for underrepresented languages.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that multilingual LLMs inherit biases from imbalanced training data distributions across languages. It extends prior research on language model safety, jailbreaking, and alignment by applying these concerns specifically to the multilingual setting. The paper connects to earlier studies examining how LLMs amplify English-centric biases and underperform on low-resource languages in various tasks. It also relates to work on adversarial robustness and safety alignment in LLMs, adapting adversarial attack methodologies like those in AdvBench to probe language-dependent vulnerabilities. The research responds to the gap in understanding how safety properties, typically evaluated in English, transfer or fail to transfer across the multilingual spectrum.
enables: This work motivates urgent research into mitigating linguistic discrimination in LLM safety. It establishes benchmarking protocols for measuring consistency across languages, enabling future work to quantify progress in closing safety gaps. The findings point toward developing language-agnostic alignment techniques, improving training data representation for low-resource languages, and creating targeted safety interventions for underrepresented linguistic communities. The paper also suggests new directions for adversarial testing that exploits language choice as an attack vector, and raises questions about equitable deployment of LLMs globally. Future work can build on these diagnostics to ensure that safety properties and human alignment remain robust regardless of query language, addressing fairness concerns in AI systems serving multilingual populations.
